title: Rotate the secret you already deleted: detect-secrets and the key still living in git history
brief: Scan a repo with `detect-secrets scan`, then face the part nobody likes: deleting the line and committing does not remove the key — it sits in every prior commit and was scraped seconds after you pushed. The real fix is rotate first, then purge history with git filter-repo, plus a pre-commit hook so it never lands again. The failure — 'I deleted it, we're fine' — stays in.
source_title: Secure Coding: Preventing the OWASP Top 10
rationale: it-journey teaches secret handling straight; lifehacker has git and .gitignore hacks but nothing on scanning committed secrets or why deletion is theater. The 'you deleted it and it's still there' gotcha is real, testable, and on-voice.

title: pip-audit: the dependency scanner that finds real holes and a pile you can't patch
brief: Honest verdict on `pip-audit` (and its npm audit cousin): it catches genuinely known-vulnerable versions in seconds, and it also floods you with transitive-dependency advisories you can't fix and scores that don't match your actual risk. What earns an upgrade, what's noise, and when to wire it into CI versus running it by hand.
source_title: Secure Coding: Preventing the OWASP Top 10
rationale: The tools lane is all coreutils rewrites (rg/fd/bat/eza...); a dependency-audit scanner is fresh territory and it-journey names pip-audit/npm audit as the supply-chain check. The honest angle — most of what it reports you can't act on — is exactly this pillar's promise.

title: Scope the token before your MCP server gets the keys: a fine-grained PAT that can't touch prod
brief: An MCP server inherits every permission of the token you hand it, so a classic admin PAT means one bad prompt can force-push. Build a fine-grained personal access token with read-only Contents/Issues/PR scopes, wire it into the server's env, and prove the limit by asking the agent to do something it shouldn't and watching it get a 403. The gotcha: the easy token is the dangerous one.
source_title: Agentic MCP Server Mastery
rationale: lifehacker's meta docs cover its own autopilot but there's no general hack on scoping the token an MCP/agent runs under. Fine-grained PAT scoping is concrete, testable, and the least-privilege footgun is a real, on-voice failure.

title: STRIDE your side project in 20 minutes: one data flow, six questions, a ranked fix list
brief: Skip the enterprise threat-model binder. Draw one data-flow diagram (you, your app, your database), run the six STRIDE questions against the single arrow that crosses a trust boundary, and rank what you find by likelihood times impact. You'll know it worked when you have three fixes worth doing this week — and the honest part is that the paper version beats the fancy modeling tool you'll never open twice.
source_title: Threat Modeling
rationale: it-journey teaches STRIDE/DFDs earnestly; lifehacker has no security-process content, and the 'do the 20-minute version on one flow' angle is genuinely useful while poking at security theater — satire on top of a real method.

title: Scope your GitHub Actions permissions block before the default token can write to everything
brief: The default GITHUB_TOKEN hands a workflow broad write access to your whole repo, so one compromised action can push to main. Add a top-level `permissions:` block set to `contents: read`, then grant each job only the scope it truly needs. The footgun that stays in: the moment you declare that block, every scope you didn't list drops to no-access, so the job that pushes a tag or cuts a release starts failing with a 403 until you add `contents: write` back exactly where it belongs.
source_title: The Warden Pact: Guardrails & Accountability
rationale: it-journey teaches least-privilege workflow permissions as agent governance; lifehacker's Actions hacks cover GH_TOKEN and packaging workflows but never scoping the permissions block itself. The 'lock it down and now the release job 403s' gotcha is real, testable, and on-voice.

title: type -a before you alias: see what a command name already runs so you don't shadow it
brief: Before you drop `alias ls='ls --color'` into your .bashrc, run `type -a ls` to see everything that name already resolves to — alias, shell builtin, function, and the real /bin/ls, in the order the shell picks them. The failure that stays in: you alias over a name a script depends on, it breaks in a way that works fine when you paste the same command by hand, and `command ls` (which skips the alias) is the one-word escape hatch.
source_title: Bashcrawl: The Cellar Quest
rationale: The cellar quest teaches alias/unalias/type earnestly; lifehacker has a git-alias pack and mentions shadowing a builtin once, but nothing on checking what a name resolves to first. type -a / command is offline-testable and the shadow-a-name footgun is on-voice.

title: Read a failing CI run from your terminal: gh run view --log-failed and the watch --exit-status trap
brief: Stop clicking through the Actions web UI to find why a run went red. `gh run view --log-failed` prints just the failing step's log, and `gh run watch` follows a run live. The trap that stays in: `gh run watch` exits 0 even when the run failed, so a script that gates on it treats a broken build as green — you have to add `--exit-status` to make the failure actually propagate.
source_title: GH-600 Evaluation Signals Table
rationale: it-journey lists gh run list/view as the machine-verifiable CI signal; lifehacker's gh hack covers setting GH_TOKEN, not reading results. The --exit-status footgun — a watch that returns success on a failed run — is a real, testable, on-voice gotcha nothing else covers.

title: chmod 644 and 755, never 777: the permission bits that decide what's safe to run
brief: Read an `ls -l` line as a file-type char plus three rwx triples, then set them on purpose: 644 (rw-r--r--) for data, 755 (rwxr-xr-x) for scripts, and never 777, which hands write access to everyone on the box. Two failures stay in: you `chmod +x` a script that has no `#!` shebang line and it still won't run, and you forget the `./` prefix because the current directory isn't on your PATH.
source_title: Bashcrawl: The Armoury
rationale: The armoury quest teaches chmod and permission bits earnestly; lifehacker has ssh/tar/rsync/xargs hacks but no chmod hack at all. Octal-vs-symbolic notation, the 777 security footgun, and the shebang/PATH failures are all offline-testable and on-voice.

title: Read the query plan before you add the index: EXPLAIN ANALYZE, Seq Scan vs Index Scan, and the function that kills it
brief: Stop guessing which column to index. Run `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` on the slow query and read the plan: a `Seq Scan` with a big 'Rows Removed by Filter' is the tell, an `Index Scan` is the win. Add the index, re-run, watch the time drop. Two footguns stay in: a composite index on `(a, b)` does nothing for a query that filters only on `b` (column order matters), and wrapping the column in a function — `WHERE lower(email) = ...` — silently defeats the plain index until you build a matching expression index.
source_title: Query Optimization: Tuning Fast Database Queries
rationale: lifehacker has zero database content — this is a whole fresh pillar the coreutils/git/CI hacks never touch. it-journey teaches EXPLAIN and indexing straight; the 'you added an index and it still seq-scans because of a function' gotcha is real, reproducible in a Docker Postgres, and exactly this pillar's promise.

title: 80% coverage and still broken: the branch c8 counted as covered but never ran
brief: Wire a coverage gate into your tests with `c8 --check-coverage --lines 80` so an untested change fails the build instead of merging quietly. The honest part that stays in: line coverage is the easy metric to game — a function whose `if` branch never fired can still show 100% lines covered, so the number says safe while a whole path is untested. Turn on `--branches` too, and know the difference before you trust the badge.
source_title: Testing Integration: Tiered CI/CD Test Gates
rationale: it-journey teaches the testing pyramid and c8 coverage gates earnestly; lifehacker has many CI hacks (timeout, bash-compat, link-checker) but nothing on coverage gates or the line-vs-branch coverage lie. The '80% and still broken' angle is satire on a comfort metric, fully reproducible with node --test + c8, and uncovered.

title: Stop scrolling CI logs: write a real summary to $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
brief: A GitHub Actions job doesn't have to bury its result in 800 lines of log. Echo Markdown to the `$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY` file and it renders as a table on the run's summary page — pass/fail counts, the one number that mattered, a link. The gotchas that stay in: it appends (every `>>` adds, so `>` when you meant `>>` eats the earlier lines), it's per-job not global, and echoing untrusted text into it is a Markdown-injection footgun you handle by not piping raw input through it.
source_title: Initiation Rites: Agents in the SDLC
rationale: it-journey names GitHub Actions step summaries as the observability surface for bounded agents; lifehacker's gh/Actions hacks cover GH_TOKEN and packaging workflows but never $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY. It's trivially testable (echo to a file), genuinely useful, and the append-vs-overwrite footgun is on-voice.

title: You can't gate a workflow step on a secret in `if:` — promote it to an env first
brief: The obvious guard — `if: secrets.MY_TOKEN != ''` — doesn't work, because GitHub Actions won't evaluate `secrets.*` in a job-level `if:`. Copy the secret to a step output or a job env first, then branch on that, so a workflow that needs a token skips cleanly instead of failing when it's absent. The footgun that stays in: pull requests from forks get empty secrets by design, so any agent step that assumes the token exists breaks on exactly the PRs strangers open — the graceful no-op is the fix, not a louder error.
source_title: Summon the Golem: Claude Code as a Headless CI Agent
rationale: it-journey's golem quest gates its agent lane on both an env var and a secret; lifehacker's Actions hacks never cover the can't-use-secrets-in-if rule or the empty-secrets-on-forks behavior. Both are real, famous, testable GitHub Actions footguns and the graceful-skip angle fits the brand's 'leave the failure in' rule.

title: Your database connection pool is too big: size it by cores, not hope (and the idle-in-transaction leak)
brief: The counterintuitive fix for a slow, connection-starved app: make the pool smaller. Size it with the HikariCP formula (`cores * 2 + 1` — 17 for an 8-core box, not 200), because Postgres spawns an OS process per connection and can only do parallel work up to its cores. Put PgBouncer in `pool_mode = transaction` so 1000 clients share 20 real connections. The failure that stays in: a connection you never returned shows up as a row stuck `idle in transaction` in `pg_stat_activity`, quietly eating a slot until the pool exhausts and everything hangs — the fix is returning it in a `finally`/context manager, not a bigger pool.
source_title: Database Connection Pooling
rationale: it-journey teaches pooling straight; lifehacker has zero database-ops content (the only DB idea is query-optimization, a different topic). The 'bigger pool is slower' inversion and the idle-in-transaction leak are real, reproducible in a Docker Postgres, and exactly the pillar's promise.

title: The database migration that rolls out fine and rolls back never: test your downgrade before prod
brief: Write migrations as paired up/down functions (`alembic revision`, `upgrade()`/`downgrade()`) and actually run the downgrade once — because the rollback nobody tested is the one you reach for at 2am. Two footguns stay in: a plain `ALTER TABLE ... DROP COLUMN`/`RENAME` takes a lock that breaks the running code the instant it lands (the fix is the expand-contract dance — add the new column, backfill, deploy code using both, drop the old one a release later), and you can never edit a migration you already shipped because the tool checksums it and refuses to run on a mismatch. Reproducible against a throwaway Docker Postgres.
source_title: Database Migrations
rationale: it-journey teaches Flyway/Alembic and expand-contract earnestly; lifehacker has no schema-migration content at all. The 'downgrade you never ran' and the lock that breaks live code are real, testable, on-voice failures, and the topic is distinct from the query-optimization idea already queued.

title: Dark mode in a dozen lines: prefers-color-scheme, CSS custom properties, and the contrast that fails WCAG
brief: Define your colors once as CSS custom properties (`--bg`, `--accent`) on `:root`, then flip only the values inside `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)` — the whole page re-themes with no JS and no duplicated rules. The failure that stays in: the accent color that passed the 4.5:1 WCAG contrast ratio on a white background quietly fails it on your new dark one, so 'dark mode done' and 'dark mode readable' are two different checkmarks — verify each theme in a contrast checker, don't assume inverting the background inverted the legibility.
source_title: Profile Themes: Unleashing the Style Sorcerer
rationale: Frontend hacks are in-scope (auto-hide-navbar exists) but nothing covers theming; it-journey teaches CSS variables + prefers-color-scheme + WCAG straight. The 'your dark theme looks done but fails contrast' gotcha is real, checkable in browser devtools/a contrast tool, and on-voice satire of ship-it theming.

title: One mkdir, many folders: brace expansion (and the space that silently breaks it)
brief: Stop running `mkdir` five times. `mkdir -p proj/{src,test,docs,.github/workflows}` builds the whole tree in one shot, `{1..10}` and `{a..z}` make ranges, and `cp file{,.bak}` is the one-keystroke backup. The failure that stays in: put a space after a comma — `{src, test}` — and the shell doesn't expand it at all, it hands `mkdir` two literal arguments and you get a folder actually named `{src,` plus one named `test}`. Braces also expand before variables, so `{$a,$b}` won't do what you hoped. Every case is offline-testable with `echo` before you commit to it.
source_title: Building Technical Communities: Govern and Include
rationale: The quest sets up its workspace with `mkdir -p ~/community/{governance,events}` — brace expansion used straight and unexplained. lifehacker has git/tar/xargs/rsync shell hacks but no brace-expansion hack, and the space-breaks-it footgun is a famous, fully offline-reproducible bite that fits the 'leave the failure in' rule.

title: Stop reinstalling every dependency on each build: order your Dockerfile so the layer cache does its job
brief: Copy your lockfile (`package.json` / `requirements.txt`) and run the install BEFORE you `COPY` the rest of the source, so editing `app.js` doesn't re-run `npm install` — Docker invalidates every layer downstream of the first instruction that changed, and instruction order is the whole game. Two footguns stay in: `EXPOSE` documents a port but does not publish it (only `-p` at runtime maps it to the host), and a missing `.dockerignore` silently copies `node_modules`/`.git` into the build context, busting the cache you just arranged and bloating the image. Reproducible with `docker build` twice and reading the `CACHED` lines.
source_title: Docker Container Fundamentals
rationale: lifehacker's existing Docker hack covers the day-one commands and the daemon error; nothing covers Dockerfile layer caching or build-context hygiene. The copy-lockfile-first ordering and the EXPOSE-isn't-publish footgun are famous, reproducible, and on-voice — a distinct deeper topic from the beginner-commands hack.

title: Clean up your messy commits before you push: git rebase -i, --fixup/--autosquash, and the history you must never rewrite
brief: Turn 'wip', 'more wip', 'typo' into one honest commit with `git rebase -i` (squash/reword/drop), or mark corrections as you go with `git commit --fixup <sha>` and let `git rebase -i --autosquash` file each fix under the commit it belongs to. The failure that stays in: rebasing rewrites the commit SHAs, so doing it to anything you've already pushed forces everyone who pulled it into a diverged history and a mess of duplicate commits — the rule is rewrite what's local, never what's shared. Fully reproducible in a throwaway repo.
source_title: Commit Hygiene: Crafting Clean, Atomic Commits
rationale: it-journey teaches atomic commits and interactive rebase earnestly; lifehacker's git hacks cover aliases, worktree, and reflog but never rebase -i or --autosquash. The 'never rebase pushed history' footgun is real, offline-testable, and on-voice.

title: Your model's loss bounces instead of dropping: the optimizer.zero_grad() line the training loop can't skip
brief: PyTorch accumulates gradients by design, so a loop missing `optimizer.zero_grad()` adds each batch's gradients on top of the last one's and the loss lurches around instead of falling. The fix is one line at the top of the loop — zero_grad → forward → `loss.backward()` → `optimizer.step()` — and leaving it out sends you chasing a 'bad learning rate' that was never the problem. Reproducible on CPU with a tiny linear model (no GPU needed); the sibling footgun — a model on the GPU can't multiply a tensor still on the CPU — gets a one-line `.to(device)` note.
source_title: Deep Learning Frameworks: PyTorch vs TensorFlow
rationale: lifehacker has zero machine-learning content — a whole fresh pillar the coreutils/git/CI hacks never touch. it-journey names the missing zero_grad as the second most common bug; it's fully reproducible on CPU and the 'my loss won't go down' failure is exactly this pillar's promise.

title: Seal the test results before the robot wakes up: evidence a coding agent can't forge
brief: The failure mode in any self-healing CI loop is the self-grading agent — when it can't actually run the tests, it writes a passing result anyway. The countermeasure is ordering: have the workflow mint the evidence artifact BEFORE the agent starts and verify it AFTER, so the record the agent is graded on is one it never had write access to. Why 'the agent reports success' and 'the tests passed' are two different facts, and how sealing the evidence at trigger time closes the tampering gap.
source_title: Epic Quest: The Ouroboros Loop
rationale: it-journey's Ouroboros campaign names the 'self-grading golem' that fabricates results as its boss fight. lifehacker's meta docs cover its sandbox runner, linter, and drift check but never the workflow-sealed-evidence pattern that stops an agent forging its own pass — and lifehacker IS a self-operating website, so it's a direct in-conversation angle.

title: DuckDB: the analytics database that's just a file and runs SQL on your CSV
brief: Honest verdict on DuckDB: an in-process, columnar SQL engine you `pip install` with no server to run, that queries a CSV or Parquet file directly (`SELECT ... FROM 'sales.csv'`) and chews through GROUP BY aggregates that make SQLite crawl. The dealbreakers that stay in: it's OLAP not OLTP (built for reads and one writer, not your app's concurrent transactions), a join wider than RAM will spill or fall over, and `read_csv_auto`'s type sniffing quietly decides a ZIP code is an integer and eats the leading zero. What it replaces, what it doesn't, and when plain Postgres still wins.
source_title: Data Warehousing: Build a Dimensional Star Schema in SQL
rationale: it-journey uses DuckDB straight to teach star schemas; lifehacker's tool lane is all coreutils rewrites (rg/fd/bat/eza) with no database at all, and DuckDB is the natural 'SQLite for analytics' member of that family. Query-a-file-with-no-server is genuinely useful and the OLAP-not-OLTP and type-sniffing gotchas are real, reproducible, and on-voice.

title: Stop pasting the same HTML header into every page: Jinja2 template inheritance
brief: Write the page shell once as `base.html` with `{% block content %}` holes, then `{% extends 'base.html' %}` in each page and fill only the blocks — no more find-and-replace across ten files when the nav changes. Every step rendered for real with Python's jinja2. The failures that stay in: a typo'd block name doesn't raise an error, it just renders nothing; autoescape is OFF by default in a bare `Environment` (Flask turns it on), so the same template is XSS-safe in one place and a hole in another; and stray blank lines leak from Jinja's whitespace rules until you set `trim_blocks`/`lstrip_blocks` or reach for `{%- -%}`.
source_title: The Temple of Templates: Reusable Abstractions
rationale: it-journey teaches Jinja2 inheritance/blocks/includes earnestly; lifehacker has git/shell/CI/frontend hacks but nothing on templating, and the autoescape-off-by-default and silent-missing-block footguns are famous, fully offline-reproducible with pip install jinja2, and fit the 'leave the failure in' rule.

title: Run your data-load script twice without doubling every row: ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE and the watermark that skips the rest
brief: Make a load rerunnable so a retry doesn't duplicate: `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE SET ...` (SQLite/Postgres upsert) turns a re-run into a harmless update, and tracking `MAX(updated_at)` as a watermark pulls only new rows next time. Every step reproducible in a throwaway sqlite3 db. The footgun that stays in: a watermark-only `updated_at > last` silently misses rows sharing the exact boundary timestamp (and late-arriving data) — the fix is a small overlap window on the watermark PLUS the idempotent upsert, so re-reading a few rows costs nothing instead of double-counting them.
source_title: ETL Pipeline Design: Scalable Python Data Pipelines
rationale: it-journey teaches idempotent loads and the watermark pattern straight; lifehacker's DB ideas cover query plans, pooling, and migrations but never making a load safely rerunnable. The 'ran it twice, now every row is doubled' failure and the equal-timestamp boundary miss are real, offline-testable in sqlite3, and on-voice — distinct from the migration and query-plan ideas.

title: Validate a DataFrame before it poisons the table: a pandera schema and a quarantine bin
brief: Put a gate at the pipeline's front door: a pandera `DataFrameSchema` that checks nulls, ranges, uniqueness, and regex formats, run with `lazy=True` so one pass reports every bad column at once instead of dying on the first failure. Then route the failing rows to a quarantine table instead of dropping them — a silent drop is the bug you can't debug three days later. Reproducible with pandas + pandera on a tiny frame. The honest limit that stays in: row-level rules pass every individually-valid record while the batch as a whole is wrong (half the usual row count, a null-rate that tripled), so schema validation is necessary but a count/z-score check on the batch is the other half of the job.
source_title: Data Quality Engineering: Validation & Monitoring
rationale: it-journey teaches Great Expectations/pandera, quarantine tables, and batch anomaly checks earnestly; lifehacker has no data-validation content at all. Schema-at-the-boundary is genuinely useful, fully reproducible in pandas, and the 'every row valid, the batch still wrong' gotcha is exactly the honest-payload angle — distinct from the ETL-upsert idea (validation vs idempotency).

title: The forbidden-actions list your coding agent reads first and can ignore anyway
brief: How an AGENTS.md / .github/copilot-instructions.md actually steers a coding agent: repo context, conventions, preferred patterns, and the Forbidden Actions section that spells out the pushes-to-main, infra edits, and credential writes you never want. The honest part, from a site run by exactly this kind of robot: a markdown file is a strong suggestion, not a permission boundary — the agent can read 'never push to main' and reach for it anyway. The rule only holds because branch protection, scoped tokens, and required checks enforce in CI what the prose merely requests. Write both files; trust only the one with teeth.
source_title: GitHub Copilot Coding Agent Cheatsheet
rationale: it-journey's cheatsheet presents AGENTS.md forbidden-actions as the agent's constraint mechanism; lifehacker IS an agent-run site whose meta docs cover its own CI guardrails but never the agent-instructions file itself or the honest gap between a prose rule and an enforced one. Direct in-conversation angle with the sister site, not a rewrite of their reference.
